objective: Complete the fake sin-eating ritual convincingly over the dead farmer's body
opposition: The family watches closely; the body is fresh; Maren must sell the performance through precise physical choreography
turn: The ritual unexpectedly works — something answers from within the accumulated contamination
consequence_cost: The paladin's consciousness, combat training, and memories slam into Maren without warning or consent

objective: Survive the overwhelming sensory and psychological onslaught of the paladin's arrival
opposition: Aldric tries to impose his will — demanding prayer, submission, control of the body he now shares
turn: Maren fights back mentally, refusing the paladin's authority despite physical collapse; vomits in the ditch
consequence_cost: Maren loses muscular control in front of the farmer's family; his reputation as a calm, competent sin-eater is destroyed

objective: Establish terms of coexistence with the hostile consciousness now sharing his skull
opposition: Aldric considers Maren morally repugnant and says so; Maren considers Aldric insufferable and invasive
turn: Aldric begins grudgingly reading the System interface to Maren — the first mediated access to what everyone else sees
consequence_cost: Maren's eleven-year blindness is suddenly narrated by someone who despises everything about him

objective: Get through a normal morning in Fenhold without anyone noticing something has changed
opposition: Aldric provides running commentary on everything: hygiene, food, moral choices, ambient System data
turn: Maren discovers Aldric can read the full interface in real-time — skills, levels, nearby threats, quest data — revealing twenty-eight years of missed information
consequence_cost: Maren's daily fraud routine is permanently disrupted; he can never return to the comfortable blindness of not knowing

objective: Establish workable boundaries with Aldric before the shared existence becomes unbearable
opposition: Aldric's grief surfaces — he is dead, had an order, had people, and is now trapped with no body and no exit
turn: Maren sees Aldric as a grieving person rather than an annoyance; sincerity enters a dynamic that was pure comedy
consequence_cost: The relationship acquires emotional weight Maren is not equipped to handle; he owes something to a dead man he didn't invite

objective: Maintain the fraud with Derry while processing Aldric's constant presence
opposition: Derry notices Maren flinching at threat notifications and glancing at quest markers he's never acknowledged
turn: Derry realizes something fundamental has changed; the trust that sustained their partnership cracks
consequence_cost: The fraud partnership begins failing because Maren's behavior no longer matches the blindness he has always performed

objective: Understand what the System now registers when it encounters Maren
opposition: The System classifies Maren as an unclassified entity and generates a bounty visible across the region
turn: The bounty number is impossibly large — orders of magnitude beyond a standard sin-eater valuation
consequence_cost: Every hero in range can see Maren's bounty; his lifelong invisibility is over

objective: Decide whether to flee Fenhold and what that costs
opposition: Derry's wife Lenna and daughter Pell cannot come; leaving splits Derry from his family
turn: Derry chooses to accompany Maren out of loyalty and the fear of what happens if Maren is caught alone and questioned about their shared fraud
consequence_cost: Derry's family is left behind; the human cost of Maren's new reality lands on someone who never chose any of this

objective: Put distance between themselves and Fenhold on hero-patrolled roads
opposition: Quest-light marks their route; heroes patrol these roads and the bounty is fresh
turn: They discover an abandoned quest pooling contamination toward a nearby settlement — Maren faces a choice between safety and intervention
consequence_cost: The detour costs time and draws System attention; choosing to help means performing a ritual he's only ever faked

objective: Clear the contamination before it reaches the settlement using a real sin-eating consumption
opposition: Maren doesn't know the actual ritual order — he has only performed the fake version for eleven years
turn: The botched consumption pulls in the assassin fragment (Vess), who arrives furious about sharing skull-space with a paladin and an incompetent host
consequence_cost: Three consciousnesses now occupy one skull; Vess's combat instincts and tactical awareness flood Maren's already overloaded senses

objective: Process the collision of two System-incompatible fragments inside a System-blind host
opposition: The paladin (holy/order) and assassin (shadow/guild) classifications are fundamentally opposed; their collision scrambles something
turn: What emerges from the collision has no System classification; Aldric goes quiet and will not describe what he sees
consequence_cost: The unclassified entity is born; the System's taxonomy fails in Maren's presence; the paladin's silence replaces his sarcasm for the first time

objective: Survive the first bounty hunters — two trained, System-equipped heroes
opposition: Maren has never fought; the heroes have combat classes, System-enhanced abilities, and targeting data
turn: Maren's body fights without his permission — Aldric's training and Vess's instincts operate while Maren experiences it blind, without System data
consequence_cost: Maren kills one hero; the first real thing he has ever done is violence; the other hero flees

objective: Process the aftermath of killing another person for the first time
opposition: Nausea, shaking hands, the specific horror of his body having done things he did not choose
turn: Aldric tries to comfort him with martial language and Maren nearly screams; Vess is coldly pragmatic about necessity
consequence_cost: Maren's desire for authenticity collides with the reality that his authenticity begins with blood; no comfort lands

objective: Rest and eat after days of running and the trauma of first killing
opposition: Maren's body rejects food; the killing sits in his muscles and his throat; Aldric and Vess argue about what to do next
turn: Derry shares bread and talks about ordinary things — his daughter learning to count, the cow, the garden — and Maren listens
consequence_cost: The emotional baseline is established: what Maren is running from and toward is measured by the weight of ordinary life

objective: Find a moment of stillness in a world that has become entirely threatening
opposition: Quest-light caught in stream water — beautiful but a constant reminder of the System's presence
turn: Aldric describes the quest-light to Maren without sarcasm for the first time — a genuine aesthetic observation shared between enemies
consequence_cost: Tenderness enters the paladin-con man relationship; something shifts that neither would acknowledge

objective: Find Thessa, the real sin-eater who works in Silt
opposition: Silt is unfamiliar territory; Maren must navigate with a bounty while asking questions about a profession he's been impersonating
turn: They locate Thessa through local knowledge — she is known but not respected, doing essential work that most people would rather not think about
consequence_cost: Maren encounters someone who has spent decades doing the real version of what he has been faking

objective: Get Thessa's help understanding the fragments and the unclassified entity
opposition: Thessa realizes Maren is a fraud and her fury is rooted in professional knowledge: contamination damage, children growing up in uncleared residue
turn: Thessa tests Maren on basic sin-eating knowledge and he fails comprehensively; she names the cost of his fraud in specific, countable terms
consequence_cost: Maren's guilt becomes concrete — eleven years of accumulated contamination in named villages; the dead farmer may be his fault

objective: Convince Thessa to help despite his fraud
opposition: Thessa considers him too dangerous and too ignorant; helping him legitimizes what he's done
turn: She refuses but delivers a warning: unclassified entities are corrected by the System, the last one left nothing of the host
consequence_cost: Maren has information and a deadline but no teacher; the warning makes his situation time-limited

objective: Understand who Vess was before she became a fragment
opposition: Vess doesn't volunteer personal history; the information breaks open when Maren asks the wrong question
turn: Vess reveals her contract target (Torren Blackhall), the massacre, and her own incompleteness — memory gaps, a missing sister's name, emotions that stop mid-feeling
consequence_cost: The fragments are revealed as damaged people with unfinished lives and moral claims, not tools or annoyances

objective: Reckon with the implications of carrying edited consciousnesses
opposition: Aldric's memory gaps are different from Vess's — his death is missing, and the absence feels surgical rather than entropic
turn: Both fragments may have been altered during consumption; the System may edit what it absorbs
consequence_cost: The question of what the System hides becomes personal and immediate — it has hidden things from the people inside Maren

objective: Enter Harrow and pass through without triggering the bounty
opposition: Harrow is saturated with heroes, System infrastructure, quest boards, scorch marks — everything optimized for the System-sighted
turn: Maren navigates with Aldric narrating and Vess rating hero lethality while Derry sweats through his shirt trying to act normal
consequence_cost: The comedy of fundamental incompatibility between Maren and this environment produces escalating absurd situations

objective: Acquire supplies and route information without revealing Maren's empty System profile
opposition: A gear vendor tries to scan Maren's class and gets nothing back — a void where a classification should be
turn: Maren's blankness is conspicuous in a town where everyone has a visible System profile; his invisibility has inverted into his most identifiable feature
consequence_cost: The strategy of anonymity fails because absence of data is itself data in a System-saturated environment

objective: Escape Harrow after a hero matches the bounty description to Maren's empty profile
opposition: A hero recognizes the specific signature of an unclassified entity — the humor evaporates into immediate pursuit
turn: Maren, Derry, and the fragments flee; the exit is messy and public
consequence_cost: Maren's face is known in a hero-heavy populated area; quiet flight is no longer viable as a survival strategy

objective: Face Caul, the hero-ranger who has caught up outside Harrow
opposition: Caul wants to talk, not fight — which is worse because Maren cannot deflect moral accusation the way he might dodge a blade
turn: Caul describes losing Haley and Renn to contamination in a village with a certified sin-eater; Maren recognizes his own fraud's pattern
consequence_cost: The moral accusation becomes personal — Maren's specific fraud may have contributed to specific deaths

objective: Survive the conversation without confessing or being destroyed by guilt
opposition: Derry's face during the conversation — he knows what Maren is hearing; Caul is measured, right, and grieving
turn: Caul gives them a day's head start toward the Crook — not mercy but procedure; he wants proper judgment, not road justice
consequence_cost: Maren is given time but no absolution; Derry's trust visibly erodes; the moral weight travels with them

objective: Find a minor quest-residue pool safe enough to practice genuine sin-eating
opposition: The road between Harrow and the Crook passes through contaminated territory; pools are unstable and unpredictable
turn: Maren locates a small pool near a creek bed and decides to attempt a real consumption using fragments of Thessa's teaching and Aldric's knowledge
consequence_cost: The decision to practice is itself a transformation — choosing to become something real rather than continuing to flee as a fraud

objective: Perform a genuine sin-eating consumption with intent and understanding
opposition: The process requires sincerity and openness to contamination that feels like pulling his own skin back; his body fights it
turn: The consumption half-works: residue partially cleared but at severe physical cost — agonizing disorientation, muscular collapse
consequence_cost: Maren can do this but badly; the gap between fake performance and real practice is visceral

objective: Recover from the partial consumption and process what Aldric saw during the attempt
opposition: Physical collapse — nausea, tremors; Aldric goes quiet again, seeing something in the System's architecture he won't name
turn: Vess talks Maren through recovery with unexpected gentleness — professional care that reveals a different side of her
consequence_cost: Fragment relationships shift: Vess moves from antagonist to unexpected caretaker; Aldric's silence deepens

objective: Demand that Aldric explain what he has been seeing from inside a System-blind host
opposition: Aldric is reluctant because what he sees is terrifying and the implications reshape everything they know
turn: Aldric describes the System's architecture: containment structure, not interface. Walls, borders, edges. Something behind it. System-blindness as immunity, not deficiency.
consequence_cost: Every prior understanding of the System, Maren's condition, and the world's nature is overturned

objective: Process the revelation and confront Aldric about his own choices
opposition: Aldric admits he may have reached for Maren deliberately — he investigated System anomalies before dying and needed a System-blind host
turn: Maren's relationship with Aldric shifts into new territory: the paladin chose this, which means Maren's crisis was partly engineered
consequence_cost: The mystery opens further rather than closing neatly — Maren is a crack in a containment wall, not a hero; Aldric's death may have been a System correction

objective: Travel toward the Crook while the friendship with Derry quietly dies
opposition: Derry is withdrawing — shorter answers, meals eaten facing the road, avoided questions about what happens next
turn: Maren sees the withdrawal with professional clarity (he reads people for a living) but refuses to name it because naming it makes it real
consequence_cost: The friendship erodes through accumulated small silences; the warmth that sustained them for years is cooling

objective: Confront or fail to confront Derry's decision to leave
opposition: Derry has made contact with Caul — not for money but for safe passage home; he tells Caul the direction they are heading
turn: Derry leaves: not dramatically, not angrily. He says Pell needs her father. He is sorry. He goes.
consequence_cost: Maren loses his oldest human connection; the last tie to his identity as a fraud is severed; Caul knows their direction

objective: Arrive at the Crook and present himself to the sin-eater community
opposition: The Crook is smaller than expected — a handful of exhausted sin-eaters in a contamination-heavy valley between dungeon mouths
turn: The community is wary and underfunded; Thessa is present; Maren's bounty has drawn dangerous attention to their gathering
consequence_cost: Maren enters a community that should be home but views him as a threat to their already precarious existence

objective: Survive the sin-eaters' examination of his fragments and the unclassified entity
opposition: Thessa argues for consuming Maren — dissolving the fragments and the unclassified thing; others object on safety grounds
turn: The examination reveals the fragments are unstable and Maren has been unconsciously redistributing contamination rather than clearing it
consequence_cost: The sin-eaters cannot agree on whether Maren is salvageable or should be destroyed; his existence is professionally judged dangerous

objective: Demonstrate enough control to avoid being consumed by his own people
opposition: A supervised consumption: proper form, Thessa correcting in real-time, every sin-eater watching
turn: Maren barely manages the ritual, but contamination decreases across a wider area than any individual sin-eater can affect
consequence_cost: Maren survives through accidental utility, not skill; Thessa begins grudgingly teaching him, recognizing a capability that challenges her own understanding

objective: Defend the Crook when Caul arrives with a full hero party following Derry's information
opposition: The Crook is a seasonal camp, not a defensible position; the sin-eaters are not fighters
turn: Sin-eaters die in the initial assault — people doing their jobs who had nothing to do with Maren's situation
consequence_cost: The human cost of Maren's existence lands directly on bystanders; people are dead because of his presence

objective: Use real sin-eating mid-combat to disrupt the heroes' System-based targeting
opposition: The quest Maren eats is too large; the consumption overloads his capacity and the unclassified entity surges
turn: Maren pulls the System's attention, temporarily blinding the heroes' targeting interface — but the overload makes the unclassified entity visible to the System
consequence_cost: Maren pushes past safe limits; the unclassified entity surges beyond his control; the System notices directly

objective: Survive the System's direct response — a structural reclassification of the entire valley
opposition: The System redesignates the Crook as a threat zone: contamination spikes, quest-light floods, threat creatures spawn from dungeon mouths
turn: Caul stops fighting Maren and starts fighting what the System sends; everyone evacuates the valley
consequence_cost: The System reveals itself as willing to sacrifice its own people to maintain classification integrity; Caul's faith fractures

objective: Reckon with the dead sin-eaters and the growing instability of the unclassified entity
opposition: Vess identifies herself as the destabilizing factor — her opposed classification makes the unclassified thing harder to contain
turn: Vess proposes separation through reversed sin-eating; the cost to her is unhosted dissipation — a slow fade
consequence_cost: The decision belongs to Vess; she chooses to leave despite having reasons to stay

objective: Perform the separation ritual and lose Vess
opposition: The ritual requires genuine sin-eating skill applied in reverse; Maren uses his ceramic bowl for real spiritual work for the first time
turn: Vess leaves the way a sound leaves a room; she releases the Blackhall contract; Aldric says nothing for a long time
consequence_cost: Maren is diminished — he loses Vess's tactical instincts, her cold comfort, her voice; the skull is quieter and lonelier

objective: Travel to the Woundmouth — the thinnest point in the System's architecture
opposition: The Woundmouth is deep in dungeon territory; quest-light bleeds rather than pools; contamination is structural and ancient
turn: Aldric guides Maren through increasingly distorted System terrain where the interface glitches and the architecture's edges become physically tangible
consequence_cost: The approach is disorienting and physically oppressive; the closer they get, the more Maren can feel the wall

objective: Perform a sin-eating on the System's wound itself — consuming the containment structure's failing point
opposition: The consumption is exponentially more demanding than any quest; Maren's body and consciousness are overwhelmed
turn: Maren sees the System interface for the first and only time — and behind it, the machinery: the System as desperate containment holding something older and wilder in check
consequence_cost: The central mystery resolves: the System is not malevolent but desperate; Maren is both the crack and the only sealant

objective: Survive the consumption and choose what to do with the understanding
opposition: The consumption nearly kills him; the choice between breaking the wall and maintaining it
turn: Maren chooses maintenance — not heroism but practicality; Aldric holds him together through stubbornness
consequence_cost: The climax resolves through choice rather than combat; Maren becomes a real sin-eater by choosing the work with full knowledge of its cost

objective: Return from the Woundmouth and find a place in the world as a real sin-eater
opposition: The world hasn't changed — quest-light pools, scorch marks, the System hums — but Maren is different
turn: Thessa finds him and acknowledges him as a colleague: not warmth but professional recognition from decades of solitary work
consequence_cost: Maren has a place for the first time — not as fraud, not as hero, but as maintenance worker who chose the job

objective: Perform a real sin-eating ritual — properly, with understanding, still blind, still accompanied
opposition: Aldric reads the interface with something gentler under the sarcasm; the bowl and postures are the same but everything underneath has changed
turn: Maren completes the ritual. It works. He sees nothing. The contamination clears.
consequence_cost: The novel closes with Maren doing the work: a janitor who knows what is under the floor and keeps doing the job anyway
